### [515] [An automatic counting algorithm for the quantification and uncertainty analysis of the number of microglial cells trainable in small and heterogeneous datasets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22974)
*L. Martino,M. M. Garcia,P. S. Paradas,E. Curbelo*

Main category: cs.CE

TL;DR: The paper introduces an automatic counting method for microglial cells in spinal cord images using a kernel-based approach, eliminating manual annotation and refining rough automatic methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a need for an efficient, accurate method to count microglial cells without requiring extensive manual training or relying on rough automatic color-based systems that fail to provide specific cell counts.

Method: The paper integrates image preprocessing for noise/artifact removal and introduces a non-linear, non-parametric kernel counter method, which requires minimal training data and hyperparameter tuning.

Result: Numerical experiments on artificial and real datasets demonstrate the method's efficiency, flexibility, and accuracy in cell counting. It also includes uncertainty estimation during predictions.

Conclusion: The method offers an automated, robust, and adaptable system for cell counting in biological images, simplifying and enhancing the counting process while addressing expert opinion variability.

Abstract: Counting immunopositive cells on biological tissues generally requires either manual annotation or (when available) automatic rough systems, for scanning signal surface and intensity in whole slide imaging. In this work, we tackle the problem of counting microglial cells in lumbar spinal cord cross-sections of rats by omitting cell detection and focusing only on the counting task. Manual cell counting is, however, a time-consuming task and additionally entails extensive personnel training. The classic automatic color-based methods roughly inform about the total labeled area and intensity (protein quantification) but do not specifically provide information on cell number. Since the images to be analyzed have a high resolution but a huge amount of pixels contain just noise or artifacts, we first perform a pre-processing generating several filtered images {(providing a tailored, efficient feature extraction)}. Then, we design an automatic kernel counter that is a non-parametric and non-linear method. The proposed scheme can be easily trained in small datasets since, in its basic version, it relies only on one hyper-parameter. However, being non-parametric and non-linear, the proposed algorithm is flexible enough to express all the information contained in rich and heterogeneous datasets as well (providing the maximum overfit if required). Furthermore, the proposed kernel counter also provides uncertainty estimation of the given prediction, and can directly tackle the case of receiving several expert opinions over the same image. Different numerical experiments with artificial and real datasets show very promising results. Related Matlab code is also provided.

</details>


<div id='eess.SP'></div>

# eess.SP [[Back]](#toc)

### [516] [FM-RME: Foundation Model Empowered Radio Map Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22231)
*Dong Yang,Yue Wang,Songyang Zhang,Yingshu Li,Zhipeng Cai,Zhi Tian*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: The paper introduces FM-RME, a foundation model for multi-dimensional radio map estimation, combining physical knowledge and data-driven approaches for superior zero-shot generalization and learning performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RME techniques fail to efficiently capture multi-dimensional and dynamic spectrum environments due to limited integration of physical prior knowledge and data efficiency.

Method: FM-RME uses a geometry-aware feature extraction module and an attention-based neural network combined with a masked self-supervised pre-training strategy for learning spectrum representations.

Result: FM-RME demonstrates excellent learning across multiple datasets and showcases zero-shot capabilities surpassing traditional RME methods.

Conclusion: FM-RME is a significant advancement, leveraging physical priors and self-supervised learning to solve multi-dimensional RME tasks with enhanced efficiency and accuracy.

Abstract: Traditional radio map estimation (RME) techniques fail to capture multi-dimensional and dynamic characteristics of complex spectrum environments. Recent data-driven methods achieve accurate RME in spatial domain, but ignore physical prior knowledge of radio propagation, limiting data efficiency especially in multi-dimensional scenarios. To overcome such limitations, we propose a new foundation model, characterized by self-supervised pre-training on diverse data for zero-shot generalization, enabling multi-dimensional radio map estimation (FM-RME). Specifically, FM-RME builds an effective synergy of two core components: a geometry-aware feature extraction module that encodes physical propagation symmetries, i.e., translation and rotation invariance, as inductive bias, and an attention-based neural network that learns long-range correlations across the spatial-temporal-spectral domains. A masked self-supervised multi-dimensional pre-training strategy is further developed to learn generalizable spectrum representations across diverse wireless environments. Once pre-trained, FM-RME supports zero-shot inference for multi-dimensional RME, including spatial, temporal, and spectral estimation, without scenario-specific retraining. Simulation results verify that FM-RME exhibits desired learning performance across diverse datasets and zero-shot generalization capabilities beyond existing RME methods.

</details>


### [517] [Scattering Transform for Auditory Attention Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23003)
*René Pallenberg,Fabrice Katzberg,Alfred Mertins,Marco Maass*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: The study proposes the use of a scattering transform as a preprocessing method in auditory attention decoding for hearing aids and compares it with established methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the cocktail party problem in hearing aids and improve auditory attention decoding using advanced preprocessing methods.

Method: The study compares a two-layer scattering transform with standard preprocessing methods, filterbanks, and spectrogram-related techniques across various datasets and neural network models.

Result: The scattering transform improves performance for subject-related conditions on the KUL dataset and partially for models and conditions on the DTU dataset when sufficient training data is available.

Conclusion: The scattering transform is effective in extracting relevant information for auditory attention decoding in specific scenarios, showcasing its potential over conventional preprocessing methods.

Abstract: The use of hearing aids will increase in the coming years due to demographic change. One open problem that remains to be solved by a new generation of hearing aids is the cocktail party problem. A possible solution is electroencephalography-based auditory attention decoding. This has been the subject of several studies in recent years, which have in common that they use the same preprocessing methods in most cases. In this work, in order to achieve an advantage, the use of a scattering transform is proposed as an alternative to these preprocessing methods. The two-layer scattering transform is compared with a regular filterbank, the synchrosqueezing short-time Fourier transform and the common preprocessing. To demonstrate the performance, the known and the proposed preprocessing methods are compared for different classification tasks on two widely used datasets, provided by the KU Leuven (KUL) and the Technical University of Denmark (DTU). Both established and new neural-network-based models, CNNs, LSTMs, and recent Transformer/graph-based models are used for classification. Various evaluation strategies were compared, with a focus on the task of classifying speakers who are unknown from the training. We show that the two-layer scattering transform can significantly improve the performance for subject-related conditions, especially on the KUL dataset. However, on the DTU dataset, this only applies to some of the models, or when larger amounts of training data are provided, as in 10-fold cross-validation. This suggests that the scattering transform is capable of extracting additional relevant information.

</details>


<div id='cs.MM'></div>

# cs.MM [[Back]](#toc)

### [518] [Decoding the Hook: A Multimodal LLM Framework for Analyzing the Hooking Period of Video Ads](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22299)
*Kunpeng Zhang,Poppy Zhang,Shawndra Hill,Amel Awadelkarim*

Main category: cs.MM

TL;DR: This paper introduces a framework using multimodal large language models and advanced sampling strategies to analyze the critical first three seconds of video ads.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of analyzing the hooking period (first 3 seconds) of video ads, which directly impacts viewer engagement and ad performance.

Method: The authors utilize transformer-based multimodal large language models with sophisticated sampling strategies to analyze visual, auditory, and textual elements during the hooking period.

Result: The framework validated on large-scale social media data shows correlations between hooking period features and performance metrics like conversion rates, proving its predictive capability.

Conclusion: This scalable framework enhances understanding of video advertisements' initial impact, assisting in the optimization of ad strategies.

Abstract: Video-based ads are a vital medium for brands to engage consumers, with social media platforms leveraging user data to optimize ad delivery and boost engagement. A crucial but under-explored aspect is the 'hooking period', the first three seconds that capture viewer attention and influence engagement metrics. Analyzing this brief window is challenging due to the multimodal nature of video content, which blends visual, auditory, and textual elements. Traditional methods often miss the nuanced interplay of these components, requiring advanced frameworks for thorough evaluation.
  This study presents a framework using transformer-based multimodal large language models (MLLMs) to analyze the hooking period of video ads. It tests two frame sampling strategies, uniform random sampling and key frame selection, to ensure balanced and representative acoustic feature extraction, capturing the full range of design elements. The hooking video is processed by state-of-the-art MLLMs to generate descriptive analyses of the ad's initial impact, which are distilled into coherent topics using BERTopic for high-level abstraction. The framework also integrates features such as audio attributes and aggregated ad targeting information, enriching the feature set for further analysis.
  Empirical validation on large-scale real-world data from social media platforms demonstrates the efficacy of our framework, revealing correlations between hooking period features and key performance metrics like conversion per investment. The results highlight the practical applicability and predictive power of the approach, offering valuable insights for optimizing video ad strategies. This study advances video ad analysis by providing a scalable methodology for understanding and enhancing the initial moments of video advertisements.

</details>


<div id='cs.DS'></div>

# cs.DS [[Back]](#toc)

### [519] [static_maps: consteval std::map and std::unordered_map Implementations in C++23](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22506)
*Isaac D. Myhal,Oliver Serang*

Main category: cs.DS

TL;DR: Efficient new versions of std::map and std::unordered_map using C++23's consteval show superior performance on compile-time key lookup.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To create faster drop-in replacements for std::map and std::unordered_map for compile-time key usage.

Method: Implemented C++23 consteval-based versions of std::map and std::unordered_map for superior compile-time performance.

Result: Achieved runtime improvements (<40%, <35%, <73%, etc.) against std::unordered_map and alternative libraries in multiple use cases.

Conclusion: The library is the fastest drop-in alternative for compile-time key lookup, outperforming state-of-the-art alternatives in many cases.

Abstract: Using consteval from C++23, we implement efficient, new versions of std::map and std::unordered_map for use when the keys are known at compile time. We demonstrate superior performance of our unordered_map on three demonstration use-cases: Lookup of elemental mass from atomic symbol, lookup of amino acid from codon, and modification of stock prices from S&P 500 ticker symbols all produced runtimes <40%, <35%, <73% of the respective runtimes of the std implementations. Our library runimes were <80%, <45%, <97% of the lookup time of Frozen, an alternative perfect hashing implementation in C++ for problems also using constexpr keys. To our knowledge, this makes our library the overall fastest drop-in (i.e., with a similar API) alternative to std::unordered_map. On one arbitrarily chosen demo, we demonstrate runtimes <35% of PTHash and <89% gperf, state-of-the-art but not drop-in hashing libraries via external tools.

</details>


### [520] [Testable Learning of General Halfspaces under Massart Noise](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22300)
*Ilias Diakonikolas,Giannis Iakovidis,Daniel M. Kane,Sihan Liu*

Main category: cs.DS

TL;DR: This paper develops the first testable learning algorithm for Massart halfspaces under Gaussian distribution, achieving near-optimal error with efficient complexity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The goal is to design a system that can simultaneously test assumptions and learn near-optimal classifiers for noisy data under Gaussian marginals.

Method: The paper introduces a tester-learner framework and a novel sandwiching polynomial approximation to achieve multiplicative error handling for the sign function.

Result: The algorithm achieves complexity of $d^{\mathrm{polylog}(\min\{1/γ, 1/ε\})}$ and meets the quasi-polynomial Statistical Query lower bounds.

Conclusion: The approach successfully combines testable learning mechanisms with high-efficiency algorithms, advancing learning theory for noisy data scenarios.

Abstract: We study the algorithmic task of testably learning general Massart halfspaces under the Gaussian distribution. In the testable learning setting, the aim is the design of a tester-learner pair satisfying the following properties: (1) if the tester accepts, the learner outputs a hypothesis and a certificate that it achieves near-optimal error, and (2) it is highly unlikely that the tester rejects if the data satisfies the underlying assumptions. Our main result is the first testable learning algorithm for general halfspaces with Massart noise and Gaussian marginals. The complexity of our algorithm is $d^{\mathrm{polylog}(\min\{1/γ, 1/ε\})}$, where $ε$ is the excess error and $γ$ is the bias of the target halfspace, which qualitatively matches the known quasi-polynomial Statistical Query lower bound for the non-testable setting. The analysis of our algorithm hinges on a novel sandwiching polynomial approximation to the sign function with multiplicative error that may be of broader interest.

</details>


<div id='physics.comp-ph'></div>

# physics.comp-ph [[Back]](#toc)

### [521] [Discovery of Interpretable Physical Laws in Materials via Language-Model-Guided Symbolic Regression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22967)
*Yifeng Guan,Chuyi Liu,Dongzhan Zhou,Lei Bai,Wan-jian Yin,Jingyuan Li,Mao Su*

Main category: physics.comp-ph

TL;DR: The paper introduces a method using large language models to efficiently identify physical laws from high-dimensional data, validating it on perovskite materials.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of deriving interpretable physical laws from complex high-dimensional data using traditional methods like symbolic regression.

Method: Leverage the embedded scientific knowledge in large language models to guide the search process for physical laws, reducing search space complexity.

Result: Novel formulas for bulk modulus, band gap, and oxygen evolution reaction activity are discovered, outperforming previous formulas in accuracy and simplicity.

Conclusion: The framework effectively mitigates traditional challenges, leading to meaningful and improved formulas for material properties.

Abstract: Discovering interpretable physical laws from high-dimensional data is a fundamental challenge in scientific research. Traditional methods, such as symbolic regression, often produce complex, unphysical formulas when searching a vast space of possible forms. We introduce a framework that guides the search process by leveraging the embedded scientific knowledge of large language models, enabling efficient identification of physical laws in the data. We validate our approach by modeling key properties of perovskite materials. Our method mitigates the combinatorial explosion commonly encountered in traditional symbolic regression, reducing the effective search space by a factor of approximately $10^5$. A set of novel formulas for bulk modulus, band gap, and oxygen evolution reaction activity are identified, which not only provide meaningful physical insights but also outperform previous formulas in accuracy and simplicity.

</details>


<div id='cs.GR'></div>

# cs.GR [[Back]](#toc)

### [522] [DiffBMP: Differentiable Rendering with Bitmap Primitives](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22625)
*Seongmin Hong,Junghun James Kim,Daehyeop Kim,Insoo Chung,Se Young Chun*

Main category: cs.GR

TL;DR: The paper presents DiffBMP, a differentiable rendering engine for bitmap images that enables optimization tasks in under 1 minute on consumer GPUs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional differentiable renderers are limited to vector graphics, leaving bitmap images underutilized.

Method: The authors developed a parallelized rendering pipeline using CUDA implementation, incorporated soft rasterization, structure-aware techniques, and used customized losses for optimization.

Result: DiffBMP efficiently handles bitmap image parameters optimization, performing tasks on thousands of primitives quickly using consumer-level hardware.

Conclusion: DiffBMP is a practical tool for creative workflows, with public availability and integration capabilities, facilitating advanced rendering and editing of bitmap images.

Abstract: We introduce DiffBMP, a scalable and efficient differentiable rendering engine for a collection of bitmap images. Our work addresses a limitation that traditional differentiable renderers are constrained to vector graphics, given that most images in the world are bitmaps. Our core contribution is a highly parallelized rendering pipeline, featuring a custom CUDA implementation for calculating gradients. This system can, for example, optimize the position, rotation, scale, color, and opacity of thousands of bitmap primitives all in under 1 min using a consumer GPU. We employ and validate several techniques to facilitate the optimization: soft rasterization via Gaussian blur, structure-aware initialization, noisy canvas, and specialized losses/heuristics for videos or spatially constrained images. We demonstrate DiffBMP is not just an isolated tool, but a practical one designed to integrate into creative workflows. It supports exporting compositions to a native, layered file format, and the entire framework is publicly accessible via an easy-to-hack Python package.

</details>


### [523] [HELMLAB: An Analytical, Data-Driven Color Space for Perceptual Distance in UI Design Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23010)
*Gorkem Yildiz*

Main category: cs.GR

TL;DR: HELMLAB is a 72-parameter analytical color space designed for UI systems, offering precise color representation, alignment improvements, and advanced utilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The need for an advanced color space that enhances perceptual organization and performance in UI design systems.

Method: Development of HELMLAB through learned transformations, matrix adjustments, Fourier hue correction, and embedded lightness adjustments for superior accuracy.

Result: HELMLAB reduces color difference assessment errors with a 20.4% improvement over existing methods, ensuring high precision.

Conclusion: HELMLAB proves to be a robust and efficient tool for color representation, suitable for wide applications in UI design.

Abstract: We present HELMLAB, a 72-parameter analytical color space for UI design systems. The forward transform maps CIE XYZ to a perceptually-organized Lab representation through learned matrices, per-channel power compression, Fourier hue correction, and embedded Helmholtz-Kohlrausch lightness adjustment. A post-pipeline neutral correction guarantees that achromatic colors map to a=b=0 (chroma < 10^-6), and a rigid rotation of the chromatic plane improves hue-angle alignment without affecting the distance metric, which is invariant under isometries. On the COMBVD dataset (3,813 color pairs), HELMLAB achieves a STRESS of 23.22, a 20.4% reduction from CIEDE2000 (29.18). Cross-validation on He et al. 2022 and MacAdam 1974 shows competitive cross-dataset performance. The transform is invertible with round-trip errors below 10^-14. Gamut mapping, design-token export, and dark/light mode adaptation utilities are included for use in web and mobile design systems.

</details>


### [524] [TopoEdit: Fast Post-Optimization Editing of Topology Optimized Structures](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22430)
*Hongrui Chen,Josephine V. Carstensen,Faez Ahmed*

Main category: cs.GR

TL;DR: This paper introduces TopoEdit, a post-optimization editor for topology optimization that allows flexible, physics-aware design modifications while preserving structural performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitations of brittle late-stage edits in topology optimization, which often compromise load paths and mechanical performance but cannot be conveniently fixed due to time-consuming re-optimizations or the risk of design drift.

Method: TopoEdit leverages structured latent embeddings from a pre-trained topology foundation model (OAT) for physics-aware modifications. It applies a noise-diffusion pipeline to encode, edit, and denoise designs, ensuring structural changes remain consistent with engineering constraints.

Result: TopoEdit achieves edits such as topology warping, lattice replacement, and no-design region enforcement effectively, maintaining mechanical performance and avoiding catastrophic design errors. It demonstrates significant speed and physical validity compared to traditional density-space editing.

Conclusion: TopoEdit successfully integrates machine learning and diffusion models to provide a novel tool for efficient, accurate, and physics-respecting post-optimization modifications in topology optimization workflows.

Abstract: Despite topology optimization producing high-performance structures, late-stage localized revisions remain brittle: direct density-space edits (e.g., warping pixels, inserting holes, swapping infill) can sever load paths and sharply degrade compliance, while re-running optimization is slow and may drift toward a qualitatively different design. We present TopoEdit, a fast post-optimization editor that demonstrates how structured latent embeddings from a pre-trained topology foundation model (OAT) can be repurposed as an interface for physics-aware engineering edits. Given an optimized topology, TopoEdit encodes it into OAT's spatial latent, applies partial noising to preserve instance identity while increasing editability, and injects user intent through an edit-then-denoise diffusion pipeline. We instantiate three edit operators: drag-based topology warping with boundary-condition-consistent conditioning updates, shell-infill lattice replacement using a lattice-anchored reference latent with updated volume-fraction conditioning, and late-stage no-design region enforcement via masked latent overwrite followed by diffusion-based recovery. A consistency-preserving guided DDIM procedure localizes changes while allowing global structural adaptation; multiple candidates can be sampled and selected using a compliance-aware criterion, with optional short SIMP refinement for warps. Across diverse case studies and large edit sweeps, TopoEdit produces intention-aligned modifications that better preserve mechanical performance and avoid catastrophic failure modes compared to direct density-space edits, while generating edited candidates in sub-second diffusion time per sample.

</details>


<div id='stat.AP'></div>

# stat.AP [[Back]](#toc)

### [525] [VAE-MS: An Asymmetric Variational Autoencoder for Mutational Signature Extraction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22239)
*Ida Egendal,Rasmus Froberg Brøndum,Dan J Woodcock,Christopher Yau,Martin Bøgsted*

Main category: stat.AP

TL;DR: This paper introduces VAE-MS, a variational autoencoder model for extracting mutational signatures in cancer, combining probabilistic methods and nonlinear modeling. It demonstrates superior performance on real cancer data over existing models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current methods for mutational signature extraction, largely reliant on NMF, are often unreliable and not clinically applicable. Enhanced solutions like neural networks or probabilistic methods show promise but lack integration for maximizing performance.

Method: The paper presents VAE-MS, which utilizes a variational autoencoder with an asymmetric design and probabilistic approaches for signature extraction. It is evaluated against state-of-the-art models like SigProfilerExtractor, MUSE-XAE, and SigneR.

Result: VAE-MS achieved more accurate reconstructions on real cancer data compared to others. Probabilistic models, such as VAE-MS and SigneR, outperformed non-probabilistic ones in reconstructive accuracy. No model had a consistent advantage in extracting signatures reliably.

Conclusion: By combining probabilistic components with nonlinear features, VAE-MS is a promising tool for analyzing mutational signatures in cancer, addressing some of the limitations of existing methods. Its software is publicly available for further use.

Abstract: Mutational signature analysis has emerged as a powerful method for uncovering the underlying biological processes driving cancer development. However, the signature extraction process, typically performed using non-negative matrix factorization (NMF), often lacks reliability and clinical applicability. To address these limitations, several solutions have been introduced, including the use of neural networks to achieve more accurate estimates and probabilistic methods to better capture natural variation in the data. In this work, we introduce a Variational Autoencoder for Mutational Signatures (VAE-MS), a novel model that leverages both an asymmetric architecture and probabilistic methods for the extraction of mutational signatures. VAE-MS is compared to with three state-of-the-art models for mutational signature extraction: SigProfilerExtractor, the NMF-based gold standard; MUSE-XAE, an autoencoder that employs an asymmetric design without probabilistic components; and SigneR, a Bayesian NMF model, to illustrate the strength in combining a nonlinear extraction with a probabilistic model. In the ability to reconstruct input data and generalize to unseen data, models with probabilistic components (VAE-MS, SigneR) dramatically outperformed models without (SigProfilerExtractor, MUSE-XAE). The NMF-baed models (SigneR, SigProfilerExtractor) had the most accurate reconstructions in simulated data, while VAE-MS reconstructed more accurately on real cancer data. Upon evaluating the ability to extract signatures consistently, no model exhibited a clear advantage over the others. Software for VAE-MS is available at https://github.com/CLINDA-AAU/VAE-MS.

</details>


<div id='cs.MA'></div>

# cs.MA [[Back]](#toc)

### [526] [QSIM: Mitigating Overestimation in Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning via Action Similarity Weighted Q-Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22786)
*Yuanjun Li,Bin Zhang,Hao Chen,Zhouyang Jiang,Dapeng Li,Zhiwei Xu*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: The paper proposes QSIM, a framework to address Q-value overestimation in cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Q-value overestimation caused by the max operator in temporal-difference target calculation hampers learning stability and policy optimality in MARL, especially due to the combinatorial explosion of joint action spaces.

Method: QSIM introduces a similarity-weighted approach that uses a structured near-greedy joint action space instead of relying on a greedy joint action. It computes targets by assigning more weight to actions behaviorally similar to the greedy choice, smoothing the Q-value estimate.

Result: QSIM integrates seamlessly with existing value decomposition methods, showing improved performance and stability. Empirical results demonstrate significant reduction in Q-value overestimation.

Conclusion: QSIM mitigates Q-value overestimation effectively, enhancing learning stability and policy performance in MARL applications.

Abstract: Value decomposition (VD) methods have achieved remarkable success in cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL). However, their reliance on the max operator for temporal-difference (TD) target calculation leads to systematic Q-value overestimation. This issue is particularly severe in MARL due to the combinatorial explosion of the joint action space, which often results in unstable learning and suboptimal policies. To address this problem, we propose QSIM, a similarity weighted Q-learning framework that reconstructs the TD target using action similarity. Instead of using the greedy joint action directly, QSIM forms a similarity weighted expectation over a structured near-greedy joint action space. This formulation allows the target to integrate Q-values from diverse yet behaviorally related actions while assigning greater influence to those that are more similar to the greedy choice. By smoothing the target with structurally relevant alternatives, QSIM effectively mitigates overestimation and improves learning stability. Extensive experiments demonstrate that QSIM can be seamlessly integrated with various VD methods, consistently yielding superior performance and stability compared to the original algorithms. Furthermore, empirical analysis confirms that QSIM significantly mitigates the systematic value overestimation in MARL. Code is available at https://github.com/MaoMaoLYJ/pymarl-qsim.

</details>


<div id='cs.HC'></div>

# cs.HC [[Back]](#toc)

### [527] [Relational Appliances: A Robot in the Refrigerator for Home-Based Health Promotion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22542)
*Timothy Bickmore,Mehdi Arjmand,Yunus Terzioglu*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: The study explores relational appliances, such as anthropomorphic robotic heads in refrigerators, for health promotion by influencing snack choices with personalized interaction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Investigate how kitchen appliances, particularly refrigerators, can be reimagined as interactive tools for promoting healthy dietary habits.

Method: A pilot study was conducted using a robotic head placed in a refrigerator. Participants interacted with the robot (either receiving health-promoting advice or general social chats) while selecting snacks during simulated TV breaks.

Result: The robotic head was found persuasive and socially engaging. Participants showed increased snack choice awareness, and improved nutritional decisions in the health-promotion condition.

Conclusion: Relational appliances have the potential to foster trust and long-term interaction, leveraging anthropomorphism to encourage better health-related behaviors in domestic contexts.

Abstract: Kitchen appliances are frequently used domestic artifacts situated at the point of everyday dietary decision making, making them a promising but underexplored site for health promotion. We explore the concept of relational appliances: everyday household devices designed as embodied social actors that engage users through ongoing, personalized interaction. We focus on the refrigerator, whose unique affordances, including a fixed, sensor-rich environment, private interaction space, and close coupling to food items, support contextualized, conversational engagement during snack choices. We present an initial exploration of this concept through a pilot study deploying an anthropomorphic robotic head inside a household refrigerator. In a home-lab apartment, participants repeatedly retrieved snacks during simulated TV "commercial breaks" while interacting with a human-sized robotic head. Participants were randomized to either a health-promotion condition, in which the robot made healthy snack recommendations, or a social-chat control condition. Outcomes included compliance with recommendations, nutritional quality of selected snacks, and psychosocial measures related to acceptance of the robot. Results suggest that participants found the robot persuasive, socially engaging, and increasingly natural over time, often describing it as helpful, aware, and companionable. Most participants reported greater awareness of their snack decisions and expressed interest in having such a robot in their own home. We discuss implications for designing relational appliances that leverage anthropomorphism, trust, and long-term human-technology relationships for home-based health promotion.

</details>


### [528] [Evaluating Zero-Shot and One-Shot Adaptation of Small Language Models in Leader-Follower Interaction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23312)
*Rafael R. Baptista,André de Lima Salgado,Ricardo V. Godoy,Marcelo Becker,Thiago Boaventura,Gustavo J. G. Lahr*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: The study benchmarks small language models (SLMs) for role classification in human-robot interaction (HRI), achieving robust performance with fine-tuning strategies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Leader-follower interaction roles in HRI are pivotal but challenging to assign in real-time due to resource constraints in mobile and assistive robots.

Method: SLMs were benchmarked using a novel dataset derived from an existing database and augmented with synthetic samples, employing prompt engineering and fine-tuning for evaluations.

Result: Zero-shot fine-tuning showed 86.66% classification accuracy with a low latency of 22.2 ms/sample, outperforming baseline and prompt-engineered methods. Performance degraded in one-shot modes due to architectural context limitations.

Conclusion: Fine-tuned SLMs are effective for role assignment in HRI while their limitations highlight trade-offs between dialogue complexity and reliability at the edge.

Abstract: Leader-follower interaction is an important paradigm in human-robot interaction (HRI). Yet, assigning roles in real time remains challenging for resource-constrained mobile and assistive robots. While large language models (LLMs) have shown promise for natural communication, their size and latency limit on-device deployment. Small language models (SLMs) offer a potential alternative, but their effectiveness for role classification in HRI has not been systematically evaluated. In this paper, we present a benchmark of SLMs for leader-follower communication, introducing a novel dataset derived from a published database and augmented with synthetic samples to capture interaction-specific dynamics. We investigate two adaptation strategies: prompt engineering and fine-tuning, studied under zero-shot and one-shot interaction modes, compared with an untrained baseline. Experiments with Qwen2.5-0.5B reveal that zero-shot fine-tuning achieves robust classification performance (86.66% accuracy) while maintaining low latency (22.2 ms per sample), significantly outperforming baseline and prompt-engineered approaches. However, results also indicate a performance degradation in one-shot modes, where increased context length challenges the model's architectural capacity. These findings demonstrate that fine-tuned SLMs provide an effective solution for direct role assignment, while highlighting critical trade-offs between dialogue complexity and classification reliability on the edge.

</details>


### [529] [TherapyProbe: Generating Design Knowledge for Relational Safety in Mental Health Chatbots Through Adversarial Simulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22775)
*Joydeep Chandra,Satyam Kumar Navneet,Yong Zhang*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: The paper introduces TherapyProbe, a methodology to assess relational safety in mental health chatbots using an adversarial multi-agent simulation to identify conversational failures and provide design recommendations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the lack of focus on multi-turn conversational safety and therapeutic dynamics in current chatbot safety evaluations, which primarily assess single-turn responses.

Method: TherapyProbe uses adversarial multi-agent simulation with open-source models to analyze chatbot conversations, identifying relational safety failures and translating them into a taxonomy with design recommendations.

Result: The authors identified 23 failure archetypes such as 'validation spirals' and 'empathy fatigue,' which were compiled into a Safety Pattern Library to inform chatbot design.

Conclusion: The methodology offers replicable tools and actionable insights for improving relational safety in chatbot interactions, benefiting developers, clinicians, and policymakers.

Abstract: As mental health chatbots proliferate to address the global treatment gap, a critical question emerges: How do we design for relational safety the quality of interaction patterns that unfold across conversations rather than the correctness of individual responses? Current safety evaluations assess single-turn crisis responses, missing the therapeutic dynamics that determine whether chatbots help or harm over time. We introduce TherapyProbe, a design probe methodology that generates actionable design knowledge by systematically exploring chatbot conversation trajectories through adversarial multi-agent simulation. Using open-source models, TherapyProbe surfaces relational safety failures interaction patterns like "validation spirals" where chatbots progressively reinforce hopelessness, or "empathy fatigue" where responses become mechanical over turns. Our contribution is translating these failures into a Safety Pattern Library of 23 failure archetypes with corresponding design recommendations. We contribute: (1) a replicable methodology requiring no API costs, (2) a clinically-grounded failure taxonomy, and (3) design implications for developers, clinicians, and policymakers.

</details>


### [530] [Addressing Climate Action Misperceptions with Generative AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22564)
*Miriam Remshard,Yara Kyrychenko,Sander van der Linden,Matthew H. Goldberg,Anthony Leiserowitz,Elena Savoia,Jon Roozenbeek*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: The paper evaluates whether a climate-informed LLM can improve individuals' understanding of impactful climate actions and increase pro-climate behavioral intentions, showing better outcomes than other methods like basic LLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the issue of misperceptions about impactful climate actions even among climate-concerned individuals, and explore innovative methods to foster behavior change to mitigate climate change.

Method: Conducted a study with 1201 climate-conscious individuals, comparing four conditions: interaction with a personalized climate-informed LLM, unspecialized LLM, web search, and no intervention.

Result: The personalized climate LLM uniquely improved understanding of climate action impacts and increased intention for behavior change, although it did not outperform a web search in knowledge acquisition.

Conclusion: LLMs equipped with climate-specific knowledge and offering personalized guidance show promise in encouraging impactful pro-climate behavior change, owing to their ability to tailor actionable advice to individuals.

Abstract: Mitigating climate change requires behaviour change. However, even climate-concerned individuals often hold misperceptions about which actions most reduce carbon emissions. We recruited 1201 climate-concerned individuals to examine whether discussing climate actions with a large language model (LLM) equipped with climate knowledge and prompted to provide personalised responses would foster more accurate perceptions of the impacts of climate actions and increase willingness to adopt feasible, high-impact behaviours. We compared this to having participants run a web search, have a conversation with an unspecialised LLM, and no intervention. The personalised climate LLM was the only condition that led to increased knowledge about the impacts of climate actions and greater intentions to adopt impactful behaviours. While the personalised climate LLM did not outperform a web search in improving understanding of climate action impacts, the ability of LLMs to deliver personalised, actionable guidance may make them more effective at motivating impactful pro-climate behaviour change.

</details>


### [531] [CoLyricist: Enhancing Lyric Writing with AI through Workflow-Aligned Support](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22606)
*Masahiro Yoshida,Bingxuan Li,Songyan Zhao,Qinyi Zhou,Shiwei Hu,Xiang Anthony Chen,Nanyun Peng*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: CoLyricist is an AI-assisted lyric tool designed to enhance lyricists' workflows and creativity through stage-specific support. User studies confirmed its effectiveness for both novice and experienced lyricists.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop a tool that supports the creative workflows of lyricists by recognizing the key stages of songwriting and addressing their challenges, which existing solutions fail to adequately cover.

Method: Semi-structured interviews with 10 experienced lyricists identified four songwriting stages (Theme Setting, Ideation, Drafting Lyrics, and Melody Fitting). CoLyricist was then designed with AI support for each stage and tested with 16 participants of varying skill levels.

Result: CoLyricist improved the songwriting experience for both novices and experts. Novices favored the melody-fitting feature, while experienced users valued ideation support.

Conclusion: CoLyricist effectively assists with lyric writing, accommodates diverse user workflows, and enhances creativity for lyricists across experience levels.

Abstract: We propose CoLyricist, an AI-assisted lyric writing tool designed to support the typical workflows of experienced lyricists and enhance their creative efficiency. While lyricists have unique processes, many follow common stages. Tools that fail to accommodate these stages challenge integration into creative practices. Existing research and tools lack sufficient understanding of these songwriting stages and their associated challenges, resulting in ineffective designs. Through a formative study involving semi-structured interviews with 10 experienced lyricists, we identified four key stages: Theme Setting, Ideation, Drafting Lyrics, and Melody Fitting. CoLyricist addresses these needs by incorporating tailored AI-driven support for each stage, optimizing the lyric writing process to be more seamless and efficient. To examine whether this workflow-aligned design also benefits those without prior experience, we conducted a user study with 16 participants, including both experienced and novice lyricists. Results showed that CoLyricist enhances the songwriting experience across skill levels. Novice users especially appreciated the Melody-Fitting feature, while experienced users valued the Ideation support.

</details>


### [532] [Simulation-based Optimization for Augmented Reading](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22735)
*Yunpeng Bai,Shengdong Zhao,Antti Oulasvirta*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: This paper introduces simulation-based optimization for augmented reading systems using resource-rational models to adapt and improve text presentation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitations of current augmented reading systems, such as dependency on heuristics, opaque models, and repeated human input, by making the process adaptive, systematic, and explainable.

Method: The authors propose resource-rational models simulating human cognitive constraints to create adaptive reading systems. They introduce offline optimization (simulated design exploration) and online optimization (real-time personalization via interaction data).

Result: The approach provides explainable and scalable augmented reading designs, reducing reliance on human testing and offering systematic optimization.

Conclusion: Simulation-based optimization grounded in resource-rational models can enable efficient, adaptive, and explainable design for augmented reading systems.

Abstract: Augmented reading systems aim to adapt text presentation to improve comprehension and task performance, yet existing approaches rely heavily on heuristics, opaque data-driven models, or repeated human involvement in the design loop. We propose framing augmented reading as a simulation-based optimization problem grounded in resource-rational models of human reading. These models instantiate a simulated reader that allocates limited cognitive resources, such as attention, memory, and time under task demands, enabling systematic evaluation of text user interfaces. We introduce two complementary optimization pipelines: an offline approach that explores design alternatives using simulated readers, and an online approach that personalizes reading interfaces in real time using ongoing interaction data. Together, this perspective enables adaptive, explainable, and scalable augmented reading design without relying solely on human testing.

</details>


### [533] [Understanding Usage and Engagement in AI-Powered Scientific Research Tools: The Asta Interaction Dataset](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.23335)
*Dany Haddad,Dan Bareket,Joseph Chee Chang,Jay DeYoung,Jena D. Hwang,Uri Katz,Mark Polak,Sangho Suh,Harshit Surana,Aryeh Tiktinsky,Shriya Atmakuri,Jonathan Bragg,Mike D'Arcy,Sergey Feldman,Amal Hassan-Ali,Rubén Lozano,Bodhisattwa Prasad Majumder,Charles McGrady,Amanpreet Singh,Brooke Vlahos,Yoav Goldberg,Doug Downey*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: The paper introduces and analyzes a large-scale user interaction dataset for AI-powered scientific research tools, unveiling how users interact with these platforms in real-world scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the lack of understanding regarding how researchers utilize AI-based scientific research tools in practice.

Method: The authors present and study the Asta Interaction Dataset, which includes over 200,000 user queries and interaction logs from two deployed AI tools. They analyze query patterns, engagement behavior, and user evolution over time.

Result: Findings include longer, complex user queries, evidence of users treating the system collaboratively, and habitual exploration of generated outputs and citations. Over time, users issued more specific queries and interacted more deeply with relevant citations.

Conclusion: The study provides a new query intent taxonomy and insights for designing effective AI research tools and benchmarks for evaluation, while releasing an anonymized dataset.

Abstract: AI-powered scientific research tools are rapidly being integrated into research workflows, yet the field lacks a clear lens into how researchers use these systems in real-world settings. We present and analyze the Asta Interaction Dataset, a large-scale resource comprising over 200,000 user queries and interaction logs from two deployed tools (a literature discovery interface and a scientific question-answering interface) within an LLM-powered retrieval-augmented generation platform. Using this dataset, we characterize query patterns, engagement behaviors, and how usage evolves with experience. We find that users submit longer and more complex queries than in traditional search, and treat the system as a collaborative research partner, delegating tasks such as drafting content and identifying research gaps. Users treat generated responses as persistent artifacts, revisiting and navigating among outputs and cited evidence in non-linear ways. With experience, users issue more targeted queries and engage more deeply with supporting citations, although keyword-style queries persist even among experienced users. We release the anonymized dataset and analysis with a new query intent taxonomy to inform future designs of real-world AI research assistants and to support realistic evaluation.

</details>


<div id='physics.acc-ph'></div>

# physics.acc-ph [[Back]](#toc)

### [534] [Advancing accelerator virtual beam diagnostics through latent evolution modeling: an integrated solution to forward, inverse, tuning, and UQ problems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22618)
*Mahindra Rautela,Alexander Scheinker*

Main category: physics.acc-ph

TL;DR: The paper introduces a Latent Evolution Model (LEM), which integrates autoencoders and transformers to model high-dimensional beam dynamics for accelerator diagnostics across different problems like forward modeling, inverse problems, and tuning challenges.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to address the traditionally separate challenges in beam diagnostics by developing a unified computational framework that handles forward modeling, inverse problems, and tuning optimization in accelerator systems.

Method: The proposed method leverages a Conditional Variational Autoencoder (CVAE) for dimensionality reduction of phase spaces, transformers for learning temporal dynamics, and neural networks for RF parameter estimation. Bayesian optimization is used for tuning RF settings.

Result: LEM successfully integrates autoencoders, transformers, and Bayesian optimization to handle multiple beam diagnostic challenges, demonstrating effective solutions for forward and inverse modeling as well as parameter tuning.

Conclusion: This unified framework combines separate diagnostic techniques into a single model, offering advancements in simulation, diagnostics, and optimization for particle accelerators.

Abstract: Virtual beam diagnostics relies on computationally intensive beam dynamics simulations where high-dimensional charged particle beams evolve through the accelerator. We propose Latent Evolution Model (LEM), a hybrid machine learning framework with an autoencoder that projects high-dimensional phase spaces into lower-dimensional representations, coupled with transformers to learn temporal dynamics in the latent space. This approach provides a common foundational framework addressing multiple interconnected challenges in beam diagnostics. For \textit{forward modeling}, a Conditional Variational Autoencoder (CVAE) encodes 15 unique projections of the 6D phase space into a latent representation, while a transformer predicts downstream latent states from upstream inputs. For \textit{inverse problems}, we address two distinct challenges: (a) predicting upstream phase spaces from downstream observations by utilizing the same CVAE architecture with transformers trained on reversed temporal sequences along with aleatoric uncertainty quantification, and (b) estimating RF settings from the latent space of the trained LEM using a dedicated dense neural network that maps latent representations to RF parameters. For \textit{tuning problems}, we leverage the trained LEM and RF estimator within a Bayesian optimization framework to determine optimal RF settings that minimize beam loss. This paper summarizes our recent efforts and demonstrates how this unified approach effectively addresses these traditionally separate challenges.

</details>


<div id='eess.SY'></div>

# eess.SY [[Back]](#toc)

### [535] [HyperKKL: Enabling Non-Autonomous State Estimation through Dynamic Weight Conditioning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22630)
*Yahia Salaheldin Shaaban,Salem Lahlou,Abdelrahman Sayed Sayed*

Main category: eess.SY

TL;DR: HyperKKL introduces a hypernetwork-based method to design KKL observers for nonlinear systems, addressing challenges of generalizing to non-autonomous dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of designing KKL observers for non-autonomous nonlinear systems, which are limited by the inability of existing solutions to handle driven dynamics without costly retraining.

Method: HyperKKL uses a hypernetwork architecture to encode exogenous inputs, enabling instant generation of KKL observer parameters and learning immersion maps for nonlinear systems.

Result: The approach is evaluated through simulations on benchmark systems (Duffing, Van der Pol, Lorenz, Rössler), showcasing its effectiveness compared to existing methods.

Conclusion: HyperKKL effectively generalizes KKL observers to non-autonomous systems, achieving practical performance without retraining or gradient updates.

Abstract: This paper proposes HyperKKL, a novel learning approach for designing Kazantzis-Kravaris/Luenberger (KKL) observers for non-autonomous nonlinear systems. While KKL observers offer a rigorous theoretical framework by immersing nonlinear dynamics into a stable linear latent space, its practical realization relies on solving Partial Differential Equations (PDE) that are analytically intractable. Current existing learning-based approximations of the KKL observer are mostly designed for autonomous systems, failing to generalize to driven dynamics without expensive retraining or online gradient updates. HyperKKL addresses this by employing a hypernetwork architecture that encodes the exogenous input signal to instantaneously generate the parameters of the KKL observer, effectively learning a family of immersion maps parameterized by the external drive. We rigorously evaluate this approach against a curriculum learning strategy that attempts to generalize from autonomous regimes via training heuristics alone. The novel approach is illustrated on four numerical simulations in benchmark examples including the Duffing, Van der Pol, Lorenz, and Rössler systems.

</details>


<div id='eess.AS'></div>

# eess.AS [[Back]](#toc)

### [536] [Deepfake Word Detection by Next-token Prediction using Fine-tuned Whisper](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22658)
*Hoan My Tran,Xin Wang,Wanying Ge,Xuechen Liu,Junichi Yamagishi*

Main category: eess.AS

TL;DR: The paper discusses fine-tuning a Whisper model for detecting deepfake speech by identifying synthetic words, achieving cost-effective results similar to dedicated detection models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Detecting synthetic speech words in deepfake audio is crucial for combating disinformation and enhancing speech security.

Method: The researchers fine-tuned a pre-trained Whisper model for next-token prediction to identify synthetic words while simultaneously transcribing speech. They used partially vocoded utterances as fine-tuning data to save on data collection costs.

Result: The fine-tuned Whisper model demonstrated low error rates in detecting synthetic words and accurate transcription for in-domain data. It performed comparably to ResNet on out-of-domain data, highlighting generalization challenges.

Conclusion: Whisper can be effectively fine-tuned for deepfake speech detection but requires improvements for handling out-of-domain synthetic speech generation models.

Abstract: Deepfake speech utterances can be forged by replacing one or more words in a bona fide utterance with semantically different words synthesized by speech generative models. While a dedicated synthetic word detector could be developed, we investigate a cost-effective method that fine-tunes a pre-trained Whisper model to detect synthetic words while transcribing the input utterance via next-token prediction. We further investigate using partially vocoded utterances as the fine-tuning data, thereby reducing the cost of data collection. Our experiments demonstrate that, on in-domain test data, the fine-tuned Whisper yields low synthetic-word detection error rates and transcription error rates. On out-of-domain test data with synthetic words produced by unseen speech generative models, the fine-tuned Whisper remains on par with a dedicated ResNet-based detection model; however, the overall performance degradation calls for strategies to improve its generalization capability.

</details>


<div id='q-bio.BM'></div>

# q-bio.BM [[Back]](#toc)

### [537] [CryoNet.Refine: A One-step Diffusion Model for Rapid Refinement of Structural Models with Cryo-EM Density Map Restraints](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.22263)
*Fuyao Huang,Xiaozhu Yu,Kui Xu,Qiangfeng Cliff Zhang*

Main category: q-bio.BM

TL;DR: CryoNet.Refine is a deep learning framework designed for rapid and automated molecular structure refinement in cryo-EM, outperforming traditional methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CryoNet.Refine addresses the challenges of computational complexity, manual tuning, and inefficiency in current cryo-EM molecular refinement methods.

Method: It employs a one-step diffusion model with density-aware loss functions and stereochemical restraints to automate structure optimization against experimental data.

Result: The framework outperforms Phenix.real_space_refine in model-map correlation and geometric quality metrics, demonstrating higher efficiency and accuracy.

Conclusion: CryoNet.Refine offers a scalable, automated solution for cryo-EM refinement, making it a valuable tool for researchers in structural biology.

Abstract: High-resolution structure determination by cryo-electron microscopy (cryo-EM) requires the accurate fitting of an atomic model into an experimental density map. Traditional refinement pipelines such as Phenix.real_space_refine and Rosetta are computationally expensive, demand extensive manual tuning, and present a significant bottleneck for researchers. We present CryoNet.Refine, an end-to-end deep learning framework that automates and accelerates molecular structure refinement. Our approach utilizes a one-step diffusion model that integrates a density-aware loss function with robust stereochemical restraints, enabling rapid optimization of a structure against experimental data. CryoNet.Refine provides a unified and versatile solution capable of refining protein complexes as well as DNA/RNA-protein complexes. In benchmarks against Phenix.real_space_refine, CryoNet.Refine consistently achieves substantial improvements in both model-map correlation and overall geometric quality metrics. By offering a scalable, automated, and powerful alternative, CryoNet.Refine aims to serve as an essential tool for next-generation cryo-EM structure refinement. Web server: https://cryonet.ai/refine; Source code: https://github.com/kuixu/cryonet.refine.

</details>
